So a problem is that the choose module does require checkm. This is because
that's how it decides which bin is the "best" from each cluster.

What is your goal with dRep? If your goal is to figure out which genomes
are similar, I would suggest using the "compare_wf" option. If your goal is
to generate a representative genome list, I would be happy to work with you
to try and figure out a way to get checkM running.

-Matt

I think the waiting pull request would have added something in that direction to scikit-image's affine warping function, which then could have been added to Affine, but so far it was not merged to scikit-image. So the only way here to do this is to manually compute scalings that don't remove image content given sampled rotation and shearing values. But would require a partial rewrite of Affine, as scale, rotation and shear are sampled independently from each other.

For PerspectiveTransform it would be even harder as that augmenter currently samples a subpolygon within the image and "zooms into" that, thereby removing anything outside of the polygon.

So I looked into it a little more, and I think it is two separate issues; one the two different unicode symbols for the angstrom symbol, and one a problem with parsing unicode units from strings in python2. On python2.7, the error looks like,


    >>> ureg('µm')
      File "<string>", line 1
        µQ_ (1 ,U_ (meter=1 ))
        ^
    SyntaxError: invalid syntax

I think it comes from the `eval` call in `pint/util.py` here:

        ret = eval(untokenize(result),
                   {'__builtins__': None},
                   {'L_': cls.from_word})

which is trying to `eval` something with unicode in it.

Disabling tests is a bad idea since it covers problems.

The bug is caused by [this line](https://github.com/joke2k/faker/blob/v0.7.2/faker/providers/date_time/__init__.py#L382) creating a value which not accepted at these platforms:
```
next_century_start = datetime(this_century_start.year + 100, 1, 1, tzinfo=tzinfo)
```
The same problem theoretically exists in `date_time_this_decade()` with `next_decade()` for the case the tests are run near the end of the supported year-range.

The solution is to use
```
next_century_start = datetime(min(this_century_start.year + 100, datetime.date.max.year),
                              1, 1, tzinfo=tzinfo)
```
Same for `date_time_this_decade()`.

Maybe you also want to issue a warning if this case occurs.

I'm picking this up after a long hiatus because I finally have some time to work on it. Sorry for taking so long on this. First off, smutt@github and hexcap@github are the same person, me, otherwise known as Andrew McConachie :) I just use hexcap@github specifically for work on hexcap. I was using smutt@github to work on another project that uses dpkt (middlebox-dane.org) but that project has quieted down and I have more time to resume work on hexcap (hexcap.org).

A lot has changed since I first wrote my code supporting 802.1q/p in dpkt. I started on my fork before dpkt was even in github. Now I think it makes sense to discard the changes in my branch WRT ethernet.py and llc.py in favor of what currently exists in kbandla@github. I would however like to merge a bugfix in igmp.py and support for Extreme Discovery Protocol(EDP) in edp.py, that are both only in my fork. Support for EDP will require an additional ethernet payload type defined in ethernet.py, but I don't believe any other changes to ethernet.py are necessary.

Given the above, I would like to close this PR and open two separate PRs. One for the bugfix in igmp.py and another to add support for EDP. I will stage these two changes in smutt@github. Once they are merged I will delete the dpkt fork in hexcap@github because there's really no reason to keep that code around anymore. Hexcap is very tightly coupled with dpkt and going forward I really don't want to maintain a separate fork of dpkt just for hexcap.

Before I do anything I wanted to post here and give the dpkt maintainers a chance to think about this and comment. What say ye? Good idea? Worst idea you've ever heard?

Also, and completely unrelated, python-dpkt is now in the OpenWRT/LEDE package repository as of a week ago and I am the maintainer.

Seems like the maximum _replen_ is a better approximation than the average _replen_ as observed for a set of reads. See below.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12987163/38667164-0ea7f3c2-3e84-11e8-8e32-e03218ec4482.png)

The merging is implemented as follows.
- the maximum _replen_ is selected
 For all regs (from different index parts) for a particular read 
- call _mm_hit_sort_by_dp_
- call _mm_set_parent_
- call _mm_select_sub_
- call _mm_set_sam_pri_
- call _mm_set_mapq_

I tested it with a long nanopore read and the comparison for a uni-part index and multi-part index looks identical except for a repeat region in chr11.

That one's going to fall in a weird space. We found that PE contamination could help quite a lot for scaffolding fragmented assemblies in our [latest paper](http://bioinformatics.oxfordjournals.org/content/32/13/1925) of BESST, so it comes as no surprise. We saw an improvement especially in the arabidopsis data, where both the contigs and the MP library have similar characteristics as yours: fragmented assembly and fairly narrow MP isize distribution (results are in the supplementary data). However, there is always a trade off between how much is scaffolded and number of errors. Furthermore, what constitutes an error is also difficult to judge, because they will come in all sizes and forms, so it depends on how stringent one wants to be and what is the downstream purpose.

I think that the assembly with PE contamination will have shorter gaps in scaffolds at least, because the PE contamination helps especially with placing shorter sequences within gaps that the MP reads leaves in scaffolds.

I've just ran into several problems with this change, which caused breaking changes with my project, though it is understandable why they occurred after reading this issue. I just wanted to say that existing projects could break due to various assumptions we used to have before this change, so perhaps this might be a heads-up for others who also found their projects breaking from today onwards.

---

Firstly, I have a base class which extends `Dict` (let's call it `BaseClass`) and some subclasses, like as follows:

```py
from addict import Dict


class BaseStrategy(Dict):
	name = None

	def __init__(self):
		super(BaseStrategy, self).__init__()

		# This line breaks because the class variable
		# name is the same as the instance variable name.
		self.name = self.__class__.name


class ExampleStrategy(BaseStrategy):
	name = 'EXAMPLE'


example = ExampleStrategy()
```

The error we get:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "poc/setattr.py", line 19, in <module>
    example = ExampleStrategy()
  File "poc/setattr.py", line 12, in __init__
    self.name = self.__class__.name
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/addict/addict.py", line 27, in __setattr__
    "'{0}' is read-only".format(name))
AttributeError: 'Dict' object attribute 'name' is read-only
```

As I found out, this is [bad practice]() so I was okay to rectify my code accordingly and rename the class variable to be a different name from my member variable.

---

Secondly, perhaps slightly more concerning, is that the new `__missing__` method creates a new instance without passing the correct arguments to `__init__`. This could break projects which assumes that addict returns `{}` for missing attributes for subclasses of Dict, or if we assume that the parent class is `addict.Dict` and therefore we can just initialise member variables through chaining:

```py
from addict import Dict


class CustomDict(Dict):
	def __init__(self, value):
		super(CustomDict, self).__init__()

		# The problem is that I do not initialize self.my as a Dict(),
		# since I previously assumed that this would work.
		self.my.custom.value = value


class CustomDict2(Dict):
	def __init__(self, value, **kwargs):
		super(CustomDict2, self).__init__()
		self.my.custom.value = value


class CustomDict3(Dict):
	def __init__(self, value=None, **kwargs):
		super(CustomDict3, self).__init__()
		self.my.custom.value = value


# Try running any of the following lines on its own
# custom_dict = CustomDict('hello world')
# custom_dict2 = CustomDict2('hello world')
# custom_dict3 = CustomDict3('hello world')

# The correct fix would be to initialize self.my as a Dict() first.


# This also breaks when I try to access a non-existent attribute:
class CustomDict4(Dict):
	def __init__(self, value, **kwargs):
		super(CustomDict4, self).__init__()
		self.value = value


custom_dict4 = CustomDict4(1)
assert not custom_dict4.missing_attr

# The correct fix would be to do:
# assert 'missing_attr' not in custom_dict4
```

Some of the errors:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "poc/getitem.py", line 26, in <module>
    custom_dict = CustomDict('hello world')
  File "poc/getitem.py", line 10, in __init__
    self.my.custom.value = value
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/addict/addict.py", line 62, in __getattr__
    return self.__getitem__(item)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/addict/addict.py", line 65, in __missing__
    return self.__class__(__parent=self, __key=name)
TypeError: __init__() got an unexpected keyword argument '__key'
```

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "poc/getitem.py", line 39, in <module>
    assert not custom_dict4.missing_attr
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/addict/addict.py", line 62, in __getattr__
    return self.__getitem__(item)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/addict/addict.py", line 65, in __missing__
    return self.__class__(__parent=self, __key=name)
TypeError: __init__() takes exactly 2 arguments (1 given)
```

Although this was a minor version bump, it may have caused quite drastic inconvenience for others as well. I'm not really sure what recommendation to give, but just wanted to mention this.

Ah, okay.  The problem is in my logic for trying to detect whether a command-line argument was explicitly set.  That `if` statement checking to see if the command-line argument value is *different* than the default value with the assumption that that will conclusively tell us whether or not the command-line argument was actually specified by the user.

In other words, if we ran

    green

Then `termcolor` would be `None` AND the argument was NOT specified on the command-line.  In this case, the logic "seems" to work, but it's just by chance.  The command really wasn't specified on the command-line, therefore checking to see if the value from argparse was the same as the default seemed to validate that it wasn't supplied on the command-line.

But it's a lie!  Because:

    green -t

Provides the exact same conditions.  But the logic that is supposed to detect whether the value came via command line falsely claims it didn't, since the value was actually provided, it just happened to be the same value as it would have been (via argparse) in the first place.

Anyway, I'm not sure if I'm making sense, but I'm aware of the actual problem and I'm trying to figure out a sane way to fix it.

When you request all the attributes for an entry the server returns only attributes that have a value, not all the attributes defined in the object classes of the returned entry, so the ldap3 library can't guess what attributes are missing. 

If you want the "record" approach (where all "fields" are always returned) you must specify what attributes you need and set the return_empty_attributes parameter to True. Or you can use the Abstraction Layer that has an object oriented approach and reads the schema and builds the relevant attribute list to request. Have a look at the tutorials at ldap3.readthedocs.io.

The method should remove duplicates, but it should not remove supersets. At least in how I understand supersets. In the example above, there was the step:
2.  `{B:2, C:42}` was updated to `{A:1,B:2,C:42}`. This entry got removed as it is now a duplicate.

For the definitions:  `{B:2, C:42} ⊂  {A:1,B:2,C:42}`, so  `{A:1,B:2,C:42}` is a superset of `{B:2, C:42}`, while  `{B:2, C:42}` is the subset.

> , altough it seems to me like it's useful in very specific cases only 

If you data is scattered between several sources (e.g. Excel files of different departments in a company), you may hit the case, where you need to merge these sources into one database. 

Or maybe I have a false understanding of how databases work. I will try to break down my problem into a minimal problem in the following. Sorry for this xy-problem ... 

---
As said above, I do have several Excel files containing information. Between these sources there exist connections by common keys. 

Source 1 contains  information "A" and "B",  Source 2  contains "A" and "C". 
So key "A" is the connection between both sources.  In total the sources  contain ~10000 entries and ~10 different keys. Sources may contain more than 2 keys and two sources may have more than just one key as connection. 

Source 1:
```
1: {A:1} 
2: {A:2, B:2}
3: {B:3}
```
Source 2:
```
1: {A:5} 
2: {A:2, C:2}
3: {C:4}
4: {A:2, C:5}
```

Task: If given any information (e.g `A==2`), create an output with all information known. 
Input: `A==2`
Output: `{A:2, B:2, C:3}; {A:2, B:2, C:2}`

My solution is/was to create one database that contains elements with all information in one database. 
```
1: {A:1} 
2: {A:2, B:2, C:2}
3: {A:2, B:2, C:3}
4: {B:3}
5: {A:5} 
6: {C:4}
```

So now you can use `db.search()` to get all the information necessary. Due to the process of this "merging" of databases it might happen that duplicates occur. Hence, my original question. 

I thought about doing something like: `{A:2, B:2, C:[2,3]}`, as this describes more the behaviour of the data in the sources. (E.g. a "A" can have several "C"s but a "C" can have at most one "A"). But then `db.search()` can't be used for key `C` anymore, if you don't know all values of C.

Another solution, that came to my mind, is the creation of a database for each source. 
Then searching in "the" database would mean to search in all databases.
Input: `C==2`
Step 1:  Search in database of source 2 and find `{A:2, C2}` 
Step 2: Search in database of source 1 for `A==2` and find `{A:2, B2}`. 
Output: `{A:2, C2}` and `{A:2, B2}`  (in some nice format)

With this solution the creation of databases is quite simple and fast.  But searching is something that takes more time and effort. So this solutions simply shifts "work" from the creation of the database to the application of the database. 
Since usually you create the basis of a database only once and later only update/insert new information, I dropped this solution. Searching is something that the user wants to do within fractions of a second. If the creation takes an hour noone minds. 

Additionally adding elements to the database is easier using one database. If the user wants to insert a new element, you can simply add it. In the second solution you need to find the correct database to insert the element. The same, but worse, problem occurs if you decide to add an additional source. How would Step 2 look like with several (new) databases? For sure it is more complicated.

---

First, please note the star `*` represents a *slice* operation equivalent to "get-all", known as `[:]` in Python notation. Also note the starting dot is redundant, i.e. `.foo.bar` is equivalent to `foo.bar`.

Now to the issue as hand. With the current `plucks` selector semantics, you *can not* pluck dictionary values when those dictionaries are inside *nested lists* (as it is in your example).

That's because each "level" of selector plucks only one-level deep, always operating at the same level. And that's to prevent unexpected plucks:
```
plucks([{'a': 1}, [{'a': 2}]], 'a')
```
should return `[1]`, not `[1,[2]]` or `[1, 2]`.

Each numeric index (`.0`) or slice selector (`.0:2`) applied to a list returns the indexed element, or a slice of the list. Each string selector (`.foo`) applied to a dicttionary, returns the value for the selected key. But when applied to a list, string selector will pluck from each element of the list, but only one-level deep. That works as expected with a list of dictionaries, but not with a list of lists with dictionaries.

One way to handle such structures would be to apply `plucks` recursively and independently to each branch/value - but than you could get unexpected results (values at different levels in input could end up on the same level in output).

The other approach would be to introduce the flatten operator. It would make sense for `*` to represent such a flattening operation. In that case `reviews.*.rating` would get you exactly what you've expected.

Thanks for pointing out this valid use case, which `plucks` could be able to handle elegantly. I'll look into the flattening operation, as that currently make the most sense to me.

I'm still stumped on the platform/version specificity, but I have a fix that hopefully avoids the issue.  The problem is actually an inconsistency in samtools.

### Short answer
 try the latest commit (933417d840f1f089c0b4990c8d7f89fd008f85df) to see if that helps.

### Long answer
 (for my own reference in the future)

Fundamentally, the problem was that the bgzipped file was being seen as length 0 by pybedtools, but there didn't seem to be anything funky with the file itself or with tabix.

I tracked it down to improper creation of a [ BAM IntervalIterator] (https://github.com/daler/pybedtools/blob/master/pybedtools/bedtool.py#L934) when it should be just a regular IntervalIterator. That is, `BedTool._isbam()` wasn't working right.

In `BedTool._isbam()` I'm differentiating a BAM from a non-bam BGZIP format file by checking the error code of `samtools view -H`.  If that command returns exit code 0, then checking the header worked -- so it must be a BAM. Non-zero exit code? Failed, so not a BAM.

And there's the problem.

```bash
# Mac OSX 10.10
samtools view -H a_non_BAM_filename   # exit = 0 (sometimes)

# Other platforms / OSX versions
samtools view -H a_non_BAM_filename   # exit = 1
```
In a platform- and OS version-specific manner (still can't reliably reproduce but only has been seen on Mac OSX 10.10 Yosemite), the that command incorrectly reports a exit code of 0.  The same command on other platforms and other Mac OSX versions correctly return non-zero exit codes and print error messages to stderr.

So the fix in 933417d840f1f089c0b4990c8d7f89fd008f85df is to avoid looking at the return code and instead look at the contents of the reported header.  Now, regardless of the exit code, the presence of an actual header means it's a BAM.

yes you can paginate your results for an inline query, refer to the bot api manual when telepot's docs are not enough, telepot is just a wrapper around them:
https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#answerinlinequery

as you can see in the manual `next_offset` key is what you want, telepot supports this paramter, from the [Reference](https://github.com/nickoala/telepot/blob/master/REFERENCE.md#telepot-Bot):
```python
answerInlineQuery(inline_query_id, results, cache_time=None, is_personal=None, next_offset=None)
```

however you are using the Answerer helper class, so we can return the `next_offset` parameter from our `compute_answer` method, we just have to return a dict:
```python
def on_inline_query(msg):
    def compute_answer():
        query_id, from_id, query_string = telepot.glance(msg, flavor='inline_query')
        print('Computing for: %s' % query_string)

        # offset is initially an empty string
        offset = int(msg['offset']) if msg['offset'] != '' else 0

        response = []

        # This function returns a list of urls pointing to gifs, all under 1mb.
        # use offset here to get the correct results, perhaps modify search_gif
        # to accept offset as an additional parameter?
        gifs = search_gif('gif {}'.format(query_string), offset)

        for id, gif in enumerate(gifs):
            # example of the first gif: https://45.media.tumblr.com/cafa4bb1b2a869b556ee57fe592213db/tumblr_o5vabtnqz21t1bfwbo7_r2_250.gif
            # i've changed this line to create unique id's
            response.append({'type': 'gif', 'id': 'gif_' + str(offset + id), 'gif_url': gif, 'thumb_url': gif})

        if len(gifs) < 10:
            # seems we ran out of gifs , set offset to '' to signal telegram
            # that we have no more results
            offset = ''
        else:
            # set offset as page number
            offset = str(offset + 1)

        print(response)
        # here instead of just sending the response, we send a dict with results and next_offset
        # you can also specify cache_time and is_personal parameters here
        return {'results': response, 'next_offset': offset}

    answerer.answer(msg, compute_answer)
```
more info on the [Answerer class in the Reference](https://github.com/nickoala/telepot/blob/master/REFERENCE.md#telepot-helper-Answerer).

The reason for the bug in pyroute2 was that the family field in the kernel's ndmsg definition is not `int32`, but `uint8` + 3 bytes pad. No idea, why it was `int32` in `pyroute2` definition :)

As a temporary solution the field is forced to be covered by a little-endian int. It works, but it's a hack.

Please don't close the issue. Instead of the endianness hack the issue must be fixed with a real definition (`uint8` + 3 bytes pad), but right now the fix has a side-effect breaking `NetNS`.

Mike, just to share this: Py3.6 supports an ANSI encoding with is basically 'bytes'. Using this encoding it's able to compress/encrypt a string, store and retrieve it binary. Not per-column yet as the intended change but quite handy. 

Config: windows10, py36, pyodbc4.0.0, mssql server 2014 express

**Example**:
import pyodbc, zlib

conn = pyodbc.connect('connection_string_here')

teststr = """Binary distributions for OS X and Windows are now uploaded to PyPI and can now be installed using pip. Source distributions are also uploaded and can be used by other platforms to compile. (PyPI only allows binary uploads for OS X and Windows.)"""
zipped = zlib.compress(teststr.encode('ansi'))

conn.setdecoding(pyodbc.SQL_CHAR, encoding='ansi')

value = zipped.decode('ansi')
cursor = conn.cursor()
cursor.execute("create table zippedcontent (zipped varchar(8000))")
cursor.execute("insert into zippedcontent (zipped) values (?)", value)
v = cursor.execute("select zipped from zippedcontent").fetchone()[0]
print(zlib.decompress(v.encode('ansi'))) # yeah!

I welcome this PR, I agree that `child` shouldn't change `db`'s state globally.

However, copying the whole Database object every time doesn't seem right. We'll end up with lots of Database instances that are not in sync. This seems like a lot of overhead we don't need.

I think a new API with a Path object, always referencing the same Database instance, could result in a nicer solution. Something like that:

```python
class Path:
    def __init__(self, db, path=''):
        self.db = db
        self.path = path
    
    def child(self, *args):
        args.insert(0, self.path)
        new_path = '/'.join([str(arg) for arg in args if arg])
        return self.__class__(self.db, new_path)
```

Here, `child` does return a new copy of itself, but keep a reference to the same Database instance.

`push`/`set`/`update`/`remove` methods should be moved to Path. We could actually change their definition to enhance chaining capabilities. Consider this:

```python
class Path:
    def __init__(self, db, path=''):
        self.db = db
        self.path = path
    
    def child(self, *args):
        args.insert(0, self.path)
        new_path = '/'.join([str(arg) for arg in args if arg])
        return self.__class__(self.db, new_path)

    def update(self, **kwargs):
        self.db.path = self.path
        self.db.update(**kwargs)
        return self
```

We can now even write: `path.child('bars/123').update({'first_name': "Foo"}).child('last_name').update("Bar")`.

It's nicer but let's take a step back and just think about `get_json_data()`

We have various situations that can occur.

1. everything is good and we return the decoded json - This is straight forward.

2. we get our json but it is broken

3. getting the url fails for some reason (no connection, times out, some error 404, 403, 500 etc)

How are we going to deal with these and what is useful for the module writer and makes their life easy.

So in my mind the first question is does the module care what went wrong?

I think the module probably doesn't care it just wants to have the data or know it hasn't

Do we want to tell the user?

Well in some occasions we probably do.  For example if we have a 404 (url not found), 403 (not authorized) because that seems like they may have not set things up correctly.  But for a 500 I think that is an issue for the website etc not our user so is just like a not available and I don't think the user needs to be informed.  We can still log stuff but not bother them.

If we are telling the user is this something that something that we can do in the function or is something that the module should decide?

I'm not sure on this one but currently I can't think of a reason.

So I'm sort of inclined to just return the decoded json or None and the helper function can log it and notify about the errors.

Now using the `get_url()` I think can allow more options for the module writer if they want more control.  I also think it should actually return a Response object that we can examine eg has `json`, `return_code`, `response` as we need.

The 'Proceed with updating your library?' dialog is the official Calibre way for plugins to insure that the GUI's in memory data cache is not corrupted by different things updating  simultaneously.

Early FanFicFare (when it was still called FanFictionDownLoader) allowed that, but was changed shortly after.  You can still see [the original discussion](https://www.mobileread.com/forums/showthread.php?p=2155484) if you like, and where I [asked about it again](https://www.mobileread.com/forums/showthread.php?p=3530867) last year during the lead up to Calibre 3.0.

If you have reason to believe that Calibre's requirements have changed since then, I'd be happy to hear about it.

Thanks for your thorough feedback. I did find destroy after some looking but it doesn't seem to be that common. To address some of the points you brought up

>You are storing authorization ("is allowed to see X") and authentication ("logged in as U") in the same session. As a result, the same information ("X can see U") is found in two different places:

>In your database (I assume it comes from there) there's some sort of record of "allow U to see X"
In the session there is now "the owner of this session is allowed to see X".

>If you change one or the other, these go out of sync. This is bad. If the administrator revoked U's priviledges to see X, they change would not be effective as long as U is able to hold onto his session.

This is partially true and you raise a valid point. In my particular case I passing my user's credentials to a authentication service and it's returning but my username, user info, and **user groups**. I've extended the flask-login with some assumptions to be able to pass groups to the `@login_required` decorator so I can say this view required a login and requires group X. This way the data is only store in memory for the period of time that the session exists

The notion of authentication revocation is there but the API is still in flux so for a workaround I've limited the session lifetime to a period that is acceptable. Ultimately there will be a background process listening and dropping auth sessions should there be a credential change. Thereby closing the loophole where a user's credentials are dropped but the session remains active.

None of them should give you the `open` price of the 1st bar. When you are in the `next` method of the strategy (or for the sake of it ... of an indicator) the point at which things are evaluated is the most recent delivered price. There is nothing looking forward for the strategy (even if the end user of the platform) knows that backtesting is taking place and the data is known in advance. As such:

  - Index `0` points to the current data
  - Index `-1` points to the previous delivered data
  - Index `-2` points to the data delivered 2 iterations ago

To get the 1st minute:

```python
    self.data.open[-len(self.data) + 1]
```

The math for the 1st iteration

  - `len(self.data) -> 1`
  - `-1 + 1 = 0`

When there is only 1 iteration the index we are looking for is `0`.

Moving to the 2nd bar:

  - `len(self.data) -> 2`
  - `-2 + 1 = -1`

We go a look at the previous delivered data which is `-1`. And the number keeps growing negative, because the 1st delivered price is further away with each iteration.

Hi, the order is not getting executed 2 days after. The log is being printed then (because the order executed when the market was closed the previous day)

In my sample above:
```python
dtstr = bt.num2date(order.executed.dt).strftime('%a %Y-%m-%d')
```

In the generic sample the printed `datetime` is a reference of when the method is being executed:
```python
 def log(self, txt, dt=None):
     ''' Logging function fot this strategy'''
     dt = dt or self.data.datetime[0]
     if isinstance(dt, float):
         dt = bt.num2date(dt)
     print('%s, %s' % (dt.isoformat(), txt))
```

The `log` method is simply logging the information when it can. The printed *datetime* is not meant to imply *execution*. As you see above you can get that with `order.executed.dt` (which is still a `float` and you can convert to a python `datetime.datetime` object using `num2date`)

> But I think we want to match [RFC 7239][1] when we're processing Forwarded. Otherwise folks will have to drop ipware when they migrate to the standardized-in-2014 Forwarded.

ipware currently fails to parse [every valid `Forwarded` value][2].  For example:

```python
from django.conf import settings
from django.http import HttpRequest
from ipware import get_client_ip

settings.IPWARE_PRIVATE_IP_PREFIX = ()
request = HttpRequest()
request.META['HTTP_FORWARDED'] = 'for=192.0.2.43, for=198.51.100.17'
get_client_ip(request)  # (None, False)
```

With this PR, we'd [successfully parse that][3].  If the goal is to scope ipware to [only handle comma-delimited IP addresses][4] and not teach it about RFC 7239, perhaps you should drop [`HTTP_FORWARDED` from the default precedence list][5]?

[1]: https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7239
[2]: https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7239#section-4
[3]: https://github.com/un33k/django-ipware/pull/35/files#diff-a01439fc3513d4a130dad113ef2f77c6R237
[4]: https://github.com/un33k/django-ipware/pull/35#issuecomment-380456511
[5]: https://github.com/un33k/django-ipware/blob/2.0.2/ipware/defaults.py#L16

I think (just my opinion) that permissions should have three parts:  what kind of permission is asked for, who is asking for permission, and for what object or route permission is asked.

- what kind of permission is covered in the argument for `@allows.requires`
- who is asking for permission is covered in the `user` object
- what permission is asked for was covered in the request, and in the new version of flask-allows is now implicit in where it is used.

Good question.

The short answer is 'no'. To give a very obvious example:

```python
>>> from arcgis2geojson import arcgis2geojson
>>> input = {
...     'x': -66.0123456789,
...     'y': 20.0123456789,
...     'spatialReference': {
...         'wkid': 4326
...     }
... }
>>> arcgis2geojson(input)
{'type': 'Point', 'coordinates': [-66.0123456789, 20.0123456789]}
```

In this case, I'm passing in an ArcGIS point object using >7 decimal places of precision and replicating them in the output GeoJSON. This violates the rule

> Don't write longitude and latitude values with more than 7 decimal places of precision.

There are probably also other violations of the spec you could point to, but that one is pretty easy to pick out and in itself makes the output non-compliant.

I do have some other thoughts, but I'm interested in your response at this point..

I see. The problem here is that explore takes a set of parameters points, and evaluates the function at that point. To do what you are trying you should mesh the two ranges first, generating all the possible pair of parameters combinations.

Say you want to explore `x E (1, 2, 3)`, and `y E (4, 5)`, you would need to create all pairs:
`[1, 4], [1, 5], [2, 4], [2, 5], [3, 4], [3, 5]`, and pass each as a parameter combination.

However notice that the number of points generated is exponential in the number of features you have - you're running a grid search, and that's precisely the problem Bayesian Optimization is trying to solve. So I urge you to not do that and, instead, increase the number of initialization points of the `maximize` method.

>1. Passing credentials as parameters in the boto.client() method

>1. Environment variables
>1. Shared credential file (~/.aws/credentials)
>1. AWS config file (~/.aws/config)
>1. Assume Role provider
>1. Boto2 config file (/etc/boto.cfg and ~/.boto)
>1. Instance metadata service on an Amazon EC2 instance that has an IAM role configured.

Hi @chhudson, consider that `process_message` will be executed on every socket message, so your `res_array` will grow during time.     def process_message(msg):

      # Insert at first position
      res_array.insert(0, msg)

      make_post_calculations(res_array)

You are absolutely right, my results above are from a limited time period, which leads to the sharp cutoff around P(alive)=0.25 corresponding to the least recent one-time customers. I guess the conclusion is that even with the fix implemented in the `ModifiedBetaGeoFitter`, the model struggles when presented with too many one time customers and the P(alive) for those customers needs to be taken with a pinch of salt.

This actually jives with another thing I've observed, namely that when including too many one-time customers, the model sometimes converges to very low values of `a` and `b`, meaning that it thinks all customers either drop out right away, or live forever. Setting initial values of 0.5 or 1.0 (instead of the default Normally distributed values) helps mitigate this.

@timbuchwaldt agreed, this is an real issue, especially when your user base is growing and those users are opening multiple sessions

I was thinking how to limit a subset of the data that is fetched from the database e.g. by filtering only active tokens or by extending model and include e.g. ip address and filter in the first place all tokens with specific ip

this is similar to your approach, but then we are loosing one functionality - self cleaning of the token table

finally what we have implemented here is pretty simple: we are using cache to resolve tokens:
- when user is accessing site first time, his token is not in cache, so there is call to the database, when successful - cache is being set
- subsequent calls are getting data from the cache itself, very fast as this is direct key access

this is very simple solution and save us a lot of hassle

one more place that can improve performance is setting cache after successful login (at token creation), to not delay cache setting till first token request,but we did not implemented it.

there was one more idea - to hash a keys in the cache itself - e.g. to get md5 or sha-xxx of the token and use it as a key in the cache - but in our situation it would not bring any additional security level and will put some more load, delaying whole process.

We completely overhauled the structure of the API wrapper with the change from version 1 to 2, with all endpoints now being linked using the hierarchy provided by the API documentation. You can manually run the transformation using the helper function `check_subscriber_hash`, which will return either the input if it matches the format of a proper hash, or check that the info provided was an email address and compute the hash of that. I see 2 solutions for your problem :

First one would be to put your backups in 2 differents folders :

> /path/to/owncloud/data/
> /path/to/owncloud/sqldump/

And put 2 differents call to rotate-backups in your crontab :

> rotate-backups [arguments] /path/to/owncloud/data/
> rotate-backups [arguments] /path/to/owncloud/sqldump/

Generally, rST substitutions are a macro. Anything within |two pipes| is expanded to the substitution.  

At our company we place common substitution definitions in a single file, and use the rst_prolog option to include that file at the beginning of every rST file.

In the attached project:

- substitions.tsr holds the substitution definition.
- body.rst, line 6, uses the substitution.
- conf.py, line 171, uses the rst_prolog configuration to place substitutions.tsr at the beginning of body.rst

The result is in _build/html/body.html

This is the function that I use to update mapfields.

```
def update_mapfield(instance, validated_data, field):
    """
    updates mongoengine MapField by iterarting over keys
    :param instance: instance to be modified (document)
    :param validated_data: dict with new data
    :param field: field to be updated
    :return: None
    """
    for key, value in validated_data.items():
        for k, v in value.items():
            if k in value:   # for making sure that the already set field doesn't get overwritten with null
                try:
                    if isinstance(instance[field][key][k], list):
                        # update_set updates the list field instead of overwriting it
                        instance[field][key][k] = update_set(instance[field][key][k], validated_data[key][k])
                    else:
                        instance[field][key][k] = validated_data[key][k]
                except KeyError:
                    instance[field].update({key: value})
    instance.save()
```

@austintrombley : I wouldn't worry about `store_tokens`, unless it is something that you know your application definitely needs to do. The average application probably doesn't need to use this feature. And using it unnecessarily or incorrectly can leave your tokens sitting around insecurely, which is bad.

One reason one might use `store_tokens` is if the application uses multiple processes or is spread out over multiple machines, and needs to coordinate auth. In that case, the process that does token refresh might need to store the new access token in a central location where other processes can access it. For example, see again our example Redis implementation, which I linked above.

Another reason might be to (securely) store the refresh token on the user's disk, that way the application can come back from a restart and continue working, without needing to redo the OAuth2 flow. One way to accomplish this would be with this library: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/keyring. The "Keychain" referred to is the secure storage on Mac OS X, which is used by keyring and other libraries/applications.

These are all examples, not advice. We're not security experts, which is why we don't try to make any suggestions about how to store tokens. Ideally, you wouldn't, unless you really have to, in which case you need to decide what is the most secure approach for your application, target platform, etc.

From the invoke docs:

> Invocations occur at least once in response to an event and functions must be idempotent to handle this.

So this means that sometimes events can trigger multiple invocations.

There's another potential concern if you're invoking synchronously in that if a retry is needed we will call invoke multiple times. If your lambda function runs longer than the socket read timeout then that will trigger a retry. You can configure the socket read timeout to be higher to avoid this, but since your function is using "Event" then this shouldn't be the problem. If this does become an issue you can use the [`Config`](https://botocore.readthedocs.io/en/stable/reference/config.html)  for your client to increase that.

You can use `boto3.set_stream_logger('botocore')` to enable debug logging, which will let you see if there are any retries going on.

> There are numerous other PaaS implementations that aren't git push centric, like Stratos (WSO2), Cloud Foundry, OpenShift, Stakato, Cloudify, but even those are easily hooked into a git repo to trigger after a git push finishes.

Just to correct you (I used to work for ActiveState), but most of them (Cloud Foundry, Stackato, OpenShift) are `git push`-centric. They just have [application manifests](http://docs.cloudfoundry.org/devguide/deploy-apps/manifest.html) which help automate a lot of the tasks people have to do in Deis. Other than that they're practically identical to Deis from the outside... plus or minus a few features here and there.

> Honestly, it feels a bit disingenuous to compare Deis to Flynn when there are so many other PaaS implementations to compare it to as well.

True, but Flynn and Deis came out at practically the same time and are both "open source docker-based PaaS"es when each came out with their PoCs. Most of the other PaaSes came well before Docker, relying on LXC directly. I think that's a valid comparison point :)

As for my contribution to the discussion (from an old Hacker News comment):

From the outside, not much. It's like comparing a toaster to another toaster. Both can toast bread, even if the internals could be completely different.  One could support a "bagel" function and one has a pretty pink accent stripe, but at the end of the day they're both toasters. ;)

Both model the Heroku-style workflow with regard to application deployment. Asides from Deis being production-ready and tacking on a few extra features like `deis pull` and Dockerfile deployment workflows, we take a different philosophical approach towards the platform and its components. We use the best-of-breed components from other OSS projects that work **today** and focus entirely on the application deployment workflow. Flynn is focused on building the platform from the ground up. They provide the network equipment and messaging primitives (layer 0, as they call it) as well as the platform itself (layer 1) completely from scratch. Deis uses common OSS tools such as `nginx` and `ceph` to provide our layer 0 and part of our layer 1 for us and focus entirely on using those components to serve our needs.

![it also got us to market faster](http://i.imgur.com/5h2v6xm.jpg)

1. Train and tests sets have to have the same dimensionality. In your example you have fewer users (30k vs 89k) in the test set, and so user 0 in the test set may not map to user 0 in the training set and so on.
2. In the binary case, you can interpret AUC as the probability that a randomly chosen positive example will be ranked higher than a randomly chosen negative example. Precision at 3 tells you what proportion of the top 3 results are positives. In your case where you have far more than 3 items, it's perfectly possible for positives to be ranked correctly overall (high AUC), but not make it to the top 3 (say, putting your positive at position 4 will give you 0.99 AUC but 0 precision@3). The results you are getting look sensible to me.
3. The prediction scores generally do not have an interpretation. They are simply a means of ranking the items.

Hello! 

I have an idea on how to implement this that I wanted to check with you, if this is still on the table. 

The idea I have (a poc implementation of) is to be able to create a new model from an old model, initialise it with random values for the item and user embeddings and biases (as ususal) and then copy the ones that we're learned in the previous model over to the new one. 

This requires two new parameters on the model, `item_feature_names` and `user_feature_names` in order to copy over the values to the correct places in the new model. 

Something along the lines of 

~~~python
    @staticmethod
    def from_model(old_model, item_feature_names, user_feature_names):
        old_model._check_initialized()
        new_model = LightFM(
            no_components=old_model.no_components,
            item_feature_names=item_feature_names,
            user_feature_names=user_feature_names
            )
        new_model._initialize(
            no_components=new_model.no_components,
            no_user_features=len(user_feature_names),
            no_item_features=len(item_feature_names))

        item_feature_idx = np.in1d(old_model.item_feature_names, new_model.item_feature_names)

        user_feature_idx = np.in1d(old_model.user_feature_names, new_model.user_feature_names)

        new_model.item_embeddings[item_feature_idx] = old_model.item_embeddings
        new_model.user_embeddings[user_feature_idx] = old_model.user_embeddings
        new_model.item_biases[item_feature_idx] = old_model.item_biases
        new_model.user_biases[user_feature_idx] = old_model.user_biases

        return new_model
~~~

Usage would be `old_model = LightFM(..., item_feature_names=item_feature_names,
    user_feature_names=user_feature_names)`, train, save the model, and then later 

~~~python
new_model = LightFM.from_model(
    old_model,
    item_feature_names=new_item_feature_names,
    user_feature_names=new_user_feature_names)
~~~

I can write this up as a proper PR of course, but wanted your input first. I'm guessing it's relevant to copy over the momentums and gradients from the old model as well.

Some comments on what I believe `pipenv` is doing around this subject, from a person relatively new to `pipenv` and using it in a Django and Docker workflow. Caveats apply to all the below, please get additional confirmation from others more familiar with the project before treating as `True`.

`pipenv install --system` instructs `pipenv` to use the system Python `site-packages`, rather than the default behavior of creating a new `virtualenv` with its own `site-packages`. This is especially preferable in a Docker deployment, to avoid any complications getting the correct Python environment set up, and in minimal containers there should not be any issues with other processes running that might get screwed up if existing packages get clobbered. The need for `sudo` depends on permissions for the `site-packages` directory. I have not required it so far, using on macOS and Debian systems, in addition to Docker, but YMMV.

The `requirements.txt` is not considered at all when using `--system` or `--deploy`. The former is instructing `pipenv` where the target `site-packages` will be, and which binaries to use; the latter is instructing `pipenv` to use the pinned versions in `Pipfile.lock` rather than attempting to run package version resolution from the version specifiers in `Pipfile`. Using `--deploy` should abort if the hash computed from `Pipfile` does not match the hash included in `Pipfile.lock`. You would use `--deploy` when your intention is to exactly replicate a deployment specified by the lock file; you would drop the `--deploy` when your intention is to create an environment that satisfies the version specifiers in `Pipfile`, and doing so implies the creation of a new `Pipfile.lock`.

Once pip with PEP 518 support is released, `setup_requires` become deprecated. So the best recommendation is for packages to declare their build dependencies in their pyproject.toml and for users to use pip 10 and install their packages that way. The pyproject.toml should ensure the build requirements are present, preventing the need for setuptools to invoke easy_install.

Also, am I right in thinking this issue only happens to very old Pythons, than 2.7.9+ supports the requisite TLS? In that case, another workaround is simply not to use old Pythons (if you need 2.7, get it from Homebrew, but really you should be using Python 3).

And if you're really in a constrained environment where you're stuck on old Pythons, consider deploying your own [devpi](http://doc.devpi.net/) instance to provide compatibility.

If it were straightforward to support TLS 1.2 in easy_install or to somehow rely on pip to install setup_requires, I'd be in support of either, but my suspicion is that either of those approaches are fraught with bootstrapping challenges, so it's not worth the effort in light of the alternatives above.
using `--assert=plain` workarounds the issue

In my opinion, the docs should represent what's recommended, the tests should guarantee what's supported, and in lieu of any tests, whatever the implementation supports is what's supported (kinda circular, I know), and we as a community are free to loosen or tighten the spec as we see fit.

I have created a patched .deb that pins the cryptography version, @marrobi @benc-uk @jwendl can you verify this resolves the issue?

```
wget https://derekb.blob.core.windows.net/public/azure-cli_2.0.14-2_all.deb
sudo dpkg -i azure-cli_2.0.14-2_all.deb
az
```

If so, we can consider pushing an update so it's available through apt-get update.

Thanks!

Hi Korijn,

Reading from an azure service bus queue will automatically block for a certain timeout period, which can be passed in as a parameter.

FYI: It's the opposite for azure storage queues, which do not block and return immediately if there are no messages.

From https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/master/azure-servicebus/azure/servicebus/servicebusservice.py#L937

```

    def receive_queue_message(self, queue_name, peek_lock=True, timeout=60):
        '''
        Receive a message from a queue for processing.

        queue_name:
            Name of the queue.
        peek_lock:
            Optional. True to retrieve and lock the message. False to read and
            delete the message. Default is True (lock).
        timeout:
            Optional. The timeout parameter is expressed in seconds.
        '''
```

As you pointed out, the docs on azure.com/readthedocs.org don't mention that though, so we should update them.

The warning is not printed by Modelsim but by VUnit.  Thus you only see it when running VUnit.

VUnit has a Verilog preprocessor since it it is necessary to perform preprocessing to scan for module dependencies since module can be defined or instantiated in a macro.  You have discovered a bug in the VUnit Verilog preprocessor. It has no consequence for you other than seeing the warning if the macro was not defining an item which effects compile dependencies which it seems your macro does not.

Here's a second explanation of the root cause of this problem, to supplement https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/7714#issuecomment-417550994.

> Start with an environment that contains `vc-14.1`, `vs2015_runtime-15.5.2`, and either `python-3.5` or `python-3.6`.  Also include conda an its dependencies.  Before we actually run the full SAT solve, we look for inconsistencies in the environment, remove those inconsistencies from the problem setup so that we can actually arrive at a solution, and then after we have a solution, put back those inconsistent packages _that aren't in the final SAT solution_.
> 
> When we revoked those packages, we caused the whole python stack to be unsatisfiable, so the current environment was completely ignored for the solution.  That means for `conda install pytz`, the python that was in the environment wasn't considered for the solution, and we therefore got the latest python (3.7).  Since python was in the final SAT solution, it got changed.  But the conda package, pycosat, and requests aren't in the final solution because they have no dependency relationship with pytz, and they were also removed from the SAT problem setup because they were considered broken.
> 
> In other words, take the broken environment, and overlay the new solution on top of it.  Thus switching out the python interpreter.

@pelson thanks hugely for your offer of help.  At this point, I am leaning towards ditching the whole feature concept for tracking compilers, and rather going back and doing it differently (hopefully right): track compiler used like numpy and python themselves are tracked.  This was originally suggested/requested by @ukoethe in https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1779#issuecomment-154563957

I don't know exactly how to do this in the most cross-platform way possible, but it certainly should not break Linux & Mac (of course).  One bonus feature to this would be to take it one step further, and match not only compiler, but also things like architecture optimized for.  For the latter, exact matches would not make sense.  An exact match would be optimal, but a less highly optimized match should also get found when an exact match is not available.  For example, let's say there's numpy with gcc5.1 and march=i686, and scipy also with gcc5.1, but with march=nehalem (numpy dependency less specifically optimized than scipy).  Installing scipy should not fail with numpy as a dependency.  GCC versions should be compatible for C APIs, but we're seeing strange crashes and numerical issues with SciPy, using the CentOS 5 GCC version.  Forcing GCC versions to match would potentially require a lot more compilation than we do now, but I think that price is worth paying for the ability to modernize more readily.

With the compiler used, one complicating factor is compatibility: the Intel compilers can be set to behave like MSVC at a particular version.  What does the compiler ID show up as when an Intel compiler is used?  How interchangeable are its produced binaries with the MSVC version that it is behaving like?  What are its runtime binary requirements?  If it needs Intel's runtime (probably), then I would argue that the Intel compiler ID needs to be intel compiler (with version) plus msvc version being "emulated" - probably not the right word, but I'm sticking with it.

If you have ideas on a quick PR for the compiler (exact matches allowed/preferred, I think), I would hugely appreciate it.  If I do it myself, I have some learning to do, but I think you are more familiar with Conda's internals and could fix this faster than me.  Bonus for the optimization stuff - but I think that's separate.  I think that's probably some hacking with MatchSpec's, in addition to encoding this optimization info somewhere.  The Intel compiler concern is a lesser one - I think that's a bit of a corner case, with not huge market share presently.  However, if the design now blocks that kind of thing later, it would be bad.

I think `man` already provides the required functionality... almost. It's only vaguely specified in `man`'s own man page:

```
       In addition, for each directory in the command search path (we'll  call
       it  a  "command  directory")  for  which  you do not have a MANPATH_MAP
       statement, man automatically looks for a manual page directory "nearby"
       namely as a subdirectory in the command directory itself or in the par-
       ent directory of the command directory.
```

On OS/X and some Python distros (Ubuntu 16), if a conda `$PREFIX/bin/` directory is in my `$PATH`, `man` seems to look in `$PREFIX/share/man/` automatically. On other Linux distros (RHEL 6), it seems to look in `$PREFIX/man/`. Apparently conda packages typically install man pages to the former, so things seem to Just Work™ on OS/X but not some Linuxes. The easiest fix might be to create a symlink to `$PREFIX/share/man` at `$PREFIX/man` on Linux, though I suppose this would break any packages that actually installed things there.

I struggled with testing, so I compared the broadband AOD measurements from TMY3 from Melbourne, FL with the lookup Linke turbidity function and the output was within an order of magnitude. The figure below shows that Linke turbidity calculated with lambda set to 500[nm] and the daily averages differ by about 10%.

![melbournefl-722040tya_linke-turb_comparison](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1385621/20734081/05abfd98-b64c-11e6-86d4-4b780e299923.png)

But the TMY3 manual says that the reported AOD is _broadband_ so if I use lambda of 700[nm] then the calculated values are much closer, but the trends deviated in autumn.

![melbournefl-722040tya_linke-turb_comparison_at700nm](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1385621/20734299/66af4ce8-b64d-11e6-8dfc-2d8444385d09.png)

 I think another test would be to compare it with ECMWF AOD/Pwat data in which the wavelength of the AOD measurement is also reported, and there are measurements at five different bands.

Here's a patch. @wholmgren and @cwhanse, not sure where you want me to put it.

```python
"""Linke turbidity factor calculated from AOD, Pwat and AM"""

from datetime import datetime
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import pvlib
from solar_utils import *
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

plt.ion()
sns.set_context(rc={'figure.figsize': (12, 8)})

def kasten_80lt(aod, am, pwat, alpha0=1.14, method='Molineaux'):
    """
    Calculate Linke turbidity factor using Kasten pyrheliometric formula (1980).

    :param aod: aerosol optical depth table or value at 500
    :param am: airmass, pressure corrected in atmospheres
    :param pwat: precipitable water or total column water vapor in centimeters
    :param alpha0: Angstrom turbidity alpha exponent, default is 1.14
    :param method: Molineaux (default) or Bird-Huldstrom
    :return: Linke turbidity

    Aerosol optical depth can be given as a list of tuples with wavelength in
    nanometers as the first item in each tuple and values as AOD as the second
    item. The list must be in ascending order by wavelength. If ``aod`` is given
    as a sequence of floats or as a float then a wavelength of 500[nm] will be
    used and alpha will default to 1.14, unless alpha is also given. Otherwise
    alpha is calculated from the given wavelength and aod.

    Method can be either ``'Molineaux'`` or ``'Bird-Huldstrom'``. Airmass less
    than 1 or greater than 6 will return ``NaN``. Precipitable water less than zero
    or greater than 5[cm] will also return ``NaN``.
    """
    # calculate Angstrom turbidity alpha exponent if not known, from AOD at two
    # wavelengths, lambda1 and lambda2
    alpha = []
    try:
        # xrange(0) means iterate zero times, xrange(negative) == xrange(0)
        for idx in xrange(len(aod) - 1):
            lambda1, aod1 = aod[idx]
            lambda2, aod2 = aod[idx + 1]
            alpha.append(-np.log(aod1 / aod2) / np.log(lambda1 / lambda2))
    except TypeError:
        # case 1: aod is a float, so len(aod) raises TypeError
        # case 2: aod is an array of float, so (lambda1, aod1) = aod[idx] raises
        # TypeError
        aod = [(500.0, aod)]
    else:
        # case 3: len(aod) == 1, then alpha == []
        if len(alpha) > 1:
            alpha0 = alpha
    # make sure alpha can be indexed
    try:
        alpha = list(alpha0)
    except TypeError:
        alpha = [alpha0]
    # make sure aod has lambda
    try:
        # case 3: len(aod) == 1 and aod == [aod]
        lambda1, aod1 = zip(*aod)
    except TypeError:
        aod = [(500.0, aod)]
    # From numerically integrated spectral simulations done with Modtran (Berk,
    # 1996), Molineaux (1998) obtained for the broadband optical depth of a
    # clean and dry atmopshere (fictious atmosphere that comprises only the
    # effects of Rayleigh scattering and absorption by the atmosphere gases
    # other than the water vapor) the following expression where am is airmass.
    delta_cda = -0.101 + 0.235 * am ** (-0.16)
    # The broadband water vapor optical depth where pwat is the precipitable
    # water vapor content of the atmosphere in [cm]. The precision of these fits
    # is better than 1% when compared with Modtran simulations in the range
    # 1 < am < 6 and 0 < pwat < 5 cm.
    delta_w = 0.112 * am ** (-0.55) * pwat ** (0.34)
    if method == 'Molineaux':
        # get aod at 700[nm] from alpha for Molineaux (1998)
        delta_a = get_aod_at_lambda(aod, alpha)
    else:
        # using (Bird-Hulstrom 1980)
        aod380 = get_aod_at_lambda(aod, alpha, 380.0)
        aod500 = get_aod_at_lambda(aod, alpha, 500.0)
        delta_a = 0.27583 * aod380 + 0.35 * aod500
    # the Linke turbidity at am using the Kasten pyrheliometric formula (1980)
    lt = -(9.4 + 0.9 * am) * np.log(
        np.exp(-am * (delta_cda + delta_w + delta_a))
    ) / am
    # filter out of extrapolated values
    filter = (amp < 1.0) | (amp > 6.0) | (pwat_cm < 0) | (pwat_cm > 5.0)
    lt[filter] = np.nan  # set out of range to NaN
    return lt


def get_aod_at_lambda(aod, alpha, lambda0=700.0):
    """
    Get AOD at specified wavelenth.

    :param aod: sequence of (wavelength, aod) in ascending order by wavelength
    :param alpha: sequence of Angstrom alpha corresponding to wavelength in aod
    :param lambda0: desired wavelength in nanometers, defaults to 700[nm]
    """
    lambda1, aod = zip(*aod)
    # lambda0 is between (idx - 1) and idx
    idx = np.searchsorted(lambda1, lambda0)
    # unless idx is zero
    if idx == 0:
        idx = 1
    return aod[idx - 1] * ((lambda0 / lambda1[idx - 1]) ** (-alpha[idx - 1]))


if __name__ == '__main__':
    melbourne_fl = pvlib.tmy.readtmy3('NREL/TMY3/722040TYA.CSV')
    aod500 = melbourne_fl[0]['AOD']
    pwat_cm = melbourne_fl[0]['Pwat']
    press_mbar = melbourne_fl[0]['Pressure']
    dry_temp = melbourne_fl[0]['DryBulb']
    timestamps = melbourne_fl[0].index
    location = (melbourne_fl[1]['latitude'],
                melbourne_fl[1]['longitude'],
                melbourne_fl[1]['TZ'])
    _, airmass = zip(*[solposAM(
        location, d.timetuple()[:6], (press_mbar.loc[d], dry_temp.loc[d])
    ) for d in timestamps])
    _, amp = zip(*airmass)
    amp = np.array(amp)
    filter = amp < 0
    amp[filter] = np.nan
    t = pd.DatetimeIndex([datetime.replace(d, year=2016) for d in timestamps])
    lt_molineaux = kasten_80lt(aod=aod500, am=amp, pwat=pwat_cm)
    lt_molineaux.index = t
    lt_bird_huldstrom = kasten_80lt(aod=aod500, am=amp, pwat=pwat_cm,
                                    method='Bird-Huldstrom')
    lt_bird_huldstrom.index = t
    pvlib.clearsky.lookup_linke_turbidity(t, *location[:2]).plot()
    lt_molineaux.resample('D').mean().plot()
    lt_bird_huldstrom.resample('D').mean().plot()
    title = ['Linke turbidity factor comparison at Melbourne, FL (722040TYA),',
             'calculated using Kasten Pyrheliometric formula']
    plt.title(' '.join(title))
    plt.ylabel('Linke turbidity factor')
    plt.legend(['Linke Turbidity', 'Molineaux', 'Bird-Huldstrom'])
```

After further digging, the issue is that we are passing fds when we need to be passing handles and then converting them to fds. The issue is that we cannot simply create handles, we also need to set them as inheritable. Much of this is not available via any public API from the stdlib but rather done internally inside subprocess and inside multiprocessing individually. See `multiprocessing.forking.duplicate` for an example.

Actually Pyramid uses WebOb which does the right thing here: https://github.com/Pylons/webob/blob/master/webob/request.py#L321 and https://github.com/Pylons/webob/blob/master/webob/request.py#L167.

Which is similar to what Werkzeug does: https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug/blob/109dad4ac9e0a1690666b2d4f29d07d98a3701d9/werkzeug/wsgi.py#L233

That being said, the encode/decode spiel is indeed correct.

Based upon the comments in the above bug reports linked by @GrahamDumpleton, it is expected that the `PATH_INFO` contains the percent decoded URL in `latin-1`. Changing this would be against the WSGI spec.

----

The only way that waitress would fix this issue is for it to accept the UTF-8, encode it, and decode it as latin-1 and put it in `PATH_INFO`, and you would still have to do the dance in your application.

After some investigation, I think it's due to the low precision that FP16 has.

When doing batch norm backward pass, gradients of `gamma` and `beta` are accumulated on a fixed channel with the forward output as input data. In this case, 1000 values are added together. 

I measure the two kinds of data difference between batchnorm op of fp64 and fp16: 
1. input data: ~0.575 total difference 
2. output data: ~3.879 total difference 

The data is obtained from the ndarrays dumped from the failing case. When doing backward pass, for each channel with 1000 addition operations, it could result in roughly 1.9*0.6=1.14 difference between fp16 and fp64 for the gradient of gamma. 

We can do two things to make the test stable and better:
1. Reduce the size of input data in the consistency test so that not too much rounding errors are accumulated. There is no need to test data with shape as big as (10, 2, 10, 10) here.
2. In the function `check_consistency`, initialize the `arg_params` and `aux_params` with the data type of the lowest precision in the `ctx_list` so that less data type casting errors are introduced while initializing executors.

I have tried the first approach with shape equal to (2, 2, 3, 3) and repeat the test more than 1000 times without failure.

If you want to directly share objects in memory, you need to be very careful about the physical layout of your structures. This means that you should use the appropriate fixed-size types, packing directives, etc. on the native side to ensure that their shape is well-defined and predictable. On the C# side, you need to match the structure shape exactly, using the same-sized shapes, as well as `LayoutKind.Sequential` or `LayoutKind.Explicit` in a [StructLayoutAttribute](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/system.runtime.interopservices.structlayoutattribute(v=vs.110).aspx).

Sharing memory like this is pretty fragile, though. Usually you would want to create a well-defined interface between your managed and native layers, and perform explicit calls between them:

C#:
```C#
[DllImport("mysharedobject")]
public static extern void GetCameraInfo(out CameraInfo cameraInfo);
```
Native:
```C
extern "C" void GetCameraInfo(CameraInfo* pCameraInfo) { return whatever; }
```

With the above, you still need to match the shape of the `CameraInfo` structure.

This is all very hand-wavy. This is a broad topic, and there are many approaches that can be taken. Given that you are talking about a "CameraInfo" structure, I'm going to say that you should avoid serializing and deserializing large amounts of state through JSON or XML.

> I don't get the reasoning behind saying clients shouldn't be checking status.

We want to avoid client devs constantly checking token validity (producing load for us, latency for their users, and more complicated code for themselves) for no real benefit because what really matters is whether a particular RS will honor a token. I also think it's also a simpler mental model for devs coming new to the system.

But it's certainly not a hard rule and I'm fine with doing it in this case. In theory it could produce issues stemming from Auth and a RS having different ideas about token validity (e.g, RS decides to reject tokens with < 2 hrs left on their lifetime because of #internalpolicy) but for the narrow set of well known services the CLI uses that's unlikely to be a real problem.

I approve of this message in principle, but I think we need to be careful to not tread on the toes of a non-Transfer API that gives non-JSON responses.
Transfer is very good about JSON everywhere, all the time, but it's not just a matter of being well behaved: other APIs may have use cases that warrant a different format.

So, my thoughts on how to do this:

- Keep the `data` property -- I like having something I can turn to and say "If a response type wants to define what all of its contents look like as a python object, do so here and only here."
- `GlobusResponse` gets a `__getitem__` method which tries to do a `__getitem__` on whatever its `data` property is. That way, non-`TransferResponse` and non-`GlobusHTTPResponse` classes can make themselves a `__getitem__` by simply ensuring that it works on `.data`. Likely that will typically a dict. Otherwise, we get a lot of same-y `__getitem__` definitions in a bunch of classes, and it gets ugly.
- If `TransferResponse` has iterable responses, that's fine, but I think it's very ugly to throw a `KeyError` if something doesn't have the `DATA` field. Either use a specialized subclass, or make iterability an option to the `TransferResponse` constructor. Attempting to iterate non-iterable responses should be a `TypeError` (just put that in `__next__`, I think?)
- Paging calls will not suddenly start returning a `TransferResponse` object -- I don't see a way of having a generic `TransferResponse` be responsible for paging without contorting things significantly. We might make `PaginatedResource` a `TransferResponse` subclass and maybe rename it.

More questions:
- Should we wrap `TypeError` on failing `__getitem__` or `__next__` in a `GlobusError` of some kind? `GlobusUsageError`?
- Do we want to do multiple-inheritance for `TransferResponse` to be a `GlobusHTTPResponse` and a `six.Iterator`, or should we just define `next` as an alias for `__next__` ourselves? It seems like a typical use case for multiple inheritance, but I don't want to have messy MRO questions down the road.


I also agree with the idea that it's time to get rid of `.json_body`.
I can't quite remember the rationale at the time for supplanting it with `.data`, other than it being shorter, but `json_body` feels fairly specific to HTTP responses.

I'm going to take a crack at this tonight in a branch, and we can discuss more tomorrow.

In scientific stuff, I beleive the most common scheme by far, is to have a separate wheel building repository, that also runs the tests.  If the tests pass, the CI uploads the wheels to the Rackspace container, and the release manager or delegate checks the wheels all built correctly, then does a scripted copy from Rackspace to PyPI.   Examples, with instructions at

* https://github.com/MacPython/numpy-wheels 
* https://github.com/MacPython/scipy-wheels

These build for Linux, OSX and Windows.  The https://github.com/pyFFTW/pyFFTW-wheels repo is built on the same [multibuild](https://github.com/matthew-brett/multibuild) machinery (which, I should say, I am mainly responsible for).

The advantage of the separate repo is that you can build / test on a wider range of platforms, without making your normal testing too slow or consuming of Travis (e.g.) resources.

For my own projects, I like to have a build / install / test wheel matrix entry in my normal repo as well, and I also run the wheel-building repository regularly by Travis-CI cron jobs.

> For the functions def_writeonly() and def_writeonly_static(), they can't be implemented using def_readwrite() and def_readwrite_static()

Sure, but they can be implemented using `def_property`, which is the generic version taking an arbitrary getter/setter (and is already called by `def_readwrite` now).  It means for a write-only attribute, you have to provide a lambda for the setter: `def_property("foo", py::none() /* or nullptr or whatever */, [](MyType &self, const double foo) { self.foo = foo; })`--so slightly more verbose, but as I said before, a bit more verbosity required for rare cases seems fine to me.

Stick to this PR.

- The `[truncated by pybind11]` part of the message isn't necessary (and worse, it just makes the string longer again).
- you want `"substring" in string`, not `string.contains("substring")`.  But I think it might be better to do a full string test here (rather than just a contains) to make sure that the truncation is happening at the correct place.
- it looks to me like the truncation string is *always* added, but should only be added when a truncation actually takes place.  Perhaps we could add a `size()` method to `class str` in `pytypes.h`:
```C++
    // Returns the string size (in unicode codepoints, *not* bytes)
    size_t size() { return (size_t) PyUnicode_GET_LENGTH(m_ptr); }
```
and then in the "invoked with" code use something like this for the shortening logic:
```C++
auto arg_repr = pybind11::repr(args_[ti]);
std::string arg =
    #ifdef NDEBUG
    arg_repr.size() <= 100 ? (std::string) arg_repr : (std::string) pybind11::str("{!r:.95}").format(arg_repr) + "[...]";
    #else
    arg_repr;
    #endif
```

I'm using 100 for the sake of example: I think we could shorten it to, e.g., 50 (with 45 in the format).  We could also use just "..." instead of "[...]", and change the format size value to `total-3` instead of `total-5`.

I think it might also be worthwhile only when `NDEBUG` is not defined so that, when compiling in debug mode, you get the entire, unaltered `repr` value (I incorporated this into the code above).

I feel like there's something essential that I don't get about this proposal. Currently, if you bind a function returning ``const std::shared_ptr<MyType>&``, the return value will be converted by ``type_caster_holder::cast`` (https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/master/include/pybind11/cast.h#L933), which will invoke ``type_caster_generic::cast`` with a pointer to the current shared pointer in the ``existing_holder`` parameter (https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/master/include/pybind11/cast.h#L262).

This function will in turn call ``detail::generic_type:: init_holder`` and then``detail::generic_type:: init_holder_helper`` (``https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/master/include/pybind11/pybind11.h#L1152``), which will initialize the shared pointer from the existing one.

And that is exactly what we want. The shared pointers know about each other, and the object won't be deallocated before all the shared pointers go out of scope.

This was solved in TRMM by minimizing the differences in reflectivity by shifting the position of the satellite data around until it matches best, then proceed with the colocation. > I think we need to specify `--upgrade` for pip to install the version from requirements.txt if Theano 0.7 is already installed.

It turns out that requiring a `0.8.git` version in the `#egg=` bit in requirements.txt solves that problem:

```
pip install Theano==0.7
echo "git+https://github.com/Theano/Theano.git@36d1eca8#egg=Theano==0.8.git" > requirements.txt
pip install -r requirements.txt  # will install from Git
```

> Thanks for making that! Some questions (some of these could be cleared up by documentation/comments - if you'd rather go that way that'd be great!)

Do you mean in the code or examples? Putting all the docs in the code might take some time and it might be better for the api to be fixed first. But good questions, to respond:

1. The reason is that the item can be made into a layer. This has many advantages, including using that layer with other layers down the track. I took the syntax from @fok's suggestion [here](https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/issues/337#issuecomment-165122642).

2. Indeed, that's exactly what it does. The current code also allows mapping the previuos output of a cell to the input of a cell, or a cell outside the graph to the input of a cell, in the third argument.

3. Not quite, the identity layer literally returns what's fed into it. This is a way to achieve a dictionary output layer using individual layers as input.

4. It's basically the following:

    for each time step:
        concatenate cell_1[t] and cell_2[t] (the concatenation is done through ConcatLayer)
        feed this into a dense recurrent layer to generate the new output

5. Ok this isn't quite what my example does. Whether init is fixed or not is set by `learn_init=False` for the entire recurrent container, which isn't really ideal. I think it's better for this to be set at the cell level and each init being optionally learnable. A possible api is:

    DenseRecurrentCell(..., W_in_to_hid=init.Uniform(), W_in_to_hid_learnable=True, ...

But that seems kind of convoluted, another approach may be to move the learnable into the init:

    DenseRecurrentCell(..., W_in_to_hid=init.Uniform(learnable=True), ...

Because `post-hook` runs after all the certs have been renewed, this would not accomplish what is needed.  What's needed is something that reports PER certificate where things are stored as it appears renew-hook does.  This would allow, for instance, the programatic installation of each certificate individually into its proper place.

In my environment, there are multiple server processes that need the certificate (eg., web and email).  I have all the certs installed into a central location and then update symlinks so each application references the latest certificate.  In some cases, a new cert is generated (for example if domains were added/removed) and the directory containing the keys and info has an extension appended (eg., -0003).   As it is, I have a script look through the directory for the highest serialization and use that as the symlink file.  

What would be better is if, after LE renewed/generated the cert files/directory, it could fire off a specified installation script, setting some environment variables to indicate, for example, what domain(s) the certificate was issued for (including SNI domains), and the directory into which the files were installed and possibly the individual filenames of the private and public keys.  That way the installation script didn't have to "guess" but would be given this info and could run it's process based on those passed values.

It also seemed to me that this would be the way to modularize cert installation and allow 3rd party "cert install" scripts to be fired off for various non-standard web servers/email servers/etc. or just more complex install scenarios rather than the LE devs needing to try to keep up with rolling them into LE.

Hopefully that makes some sense...  Since I have something that mostly works and hasn't caused grief (yet), I haven't thought about this for a while.
